fact: May 15 is exactly 135 days into the year - that's like counting to 135 while hopping on one foot!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for baby animals to be born because the weather is getting warmer and food is everywhere.
Dandelions are usually going crazy right now, shooting their fluffy seed parachutes into the air like nature's confetti cannons.
The sun is climbing higher in the sky each day in northern regions, making shadows shorter and giving you more vitamin D just by playing outside.
May 15 falls in the middle of Taurus season, which means the zodiac bull is stomping around until May 20th.
Earthworms are often having a field day right now because the soil is the perfect temperature and moisture for them to wiggle around in many temperate climates.
In many northern places, this date typically marks when the last frost danger finally goes away, so gardeners can plant their tomatoes without worrying about frozen veggie popsicles.
The Northern Hemisphere is tilting toward the sun like it's trying to get a better tan, making days longer and nights shorter.
Bees are usually buzzing around like tiny flying vacuum cleaners, collecting pollen from all the flowers that are blooming their heads off right now in temperate regions.
In the Southern Hemisphere, May 15 is deep in autumn, so leaves are typically turning colors and falling like nature's own rainbow confetti.
Morning dew often forms more easily around this time in many places because nights are still cool but days are warm, creating the perfect recipe for tiny water pearls on grass.
